ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for salted-egg-yolk fried prawns. FOODON_03315595 (shrimp food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Shrimps and prawns) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for salted-egg-yolk fried prawn preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 (Prawn) is used as the closest parent substance concept; no specific concept for salted egg yolk fried prawns exists.

preparation_method: tossed in salted egg yolk sauce
garnished with chilli padi and curry leaves

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical shared restaurant serving of golden salted egg prawns is approximately 200 g, comprising 4–6 whole prawns coated in salted egg yolk sauce with toasted cereal, chilli padi, and curry leaves.

nutrient_source.title: Fried prawn / shrimp with egg coating — estimated from USDA FoodData Central (Shrimp, cooked) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD salted egg yolk sauce component data, with cereal coating adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for salted-egg-yolk fried prawns as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining: (1) USDA FoodData Central NDB 15152 — shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (per 100 g: energy 99 kcal, protein 20.9 g, fat 1.1 g, carbohydrate 0.9 g); (2) salted duck egg yolk contribution (high fat, high sodium) from IMR Malaysia MyFCD reference data; (3) cereal coating and deep-frying oil absorption adjustment based on FAO/INFOODS SEA fried seafood analogue. Final composite per-100 g values reflect a typical restaurant preparation with approximately 40% prawn flesh, 25% salted egg yolk sauce, 20% cereal coating, and 15% absorbed frying oil and garnish. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fried seafood with coating — analogous estimate based on battered/crumbed fried seafood (GI ~48) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted-egg-yolk fried prawns. GI of 48 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for battered/crumbed fried seafood products (GI range 45–52, representative value 48), which share a similar cereal-coating and deep-frying preparation profile. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 21.6) / 100 = 10.4, based on 200 g serving with 10.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salted duck egg yolk and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content of approximately 620 mg per 100 g (1,240 mg per typical 200 g serving), which represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from cereal coating combined with high fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in small portions; the cereal coating contributes moderate carbohydrates (approximately 10.8 g per 100 g) with a low GI of 48, but the high total fat and caloric density (218 kcal per 100 g) may impair postprandial glycaemic control and contribute to weight gain if consumed frequently.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat, high cholesterol, and high sodium from salted egg yolk and deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its high saturated fat content (5.8 g per 100 g), elevated cholesterol (approximately 185 mg per 100 g from prawn and salted egg yolk), and high sodium, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawns Fried with Golden Salted Egg is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean Zi Char dish in which whole prawns are deep-fried until crispy and then tossed in a rich, aromatic sauce made from steamed and mashed salted duck egg yolks, butter, evaporated milk, and a touch of sugar. A defining feature of this restaurant-style preparation is the addition of toasted oat-based cereal, which provides an extra layer of crunch and a nutty flavour dimension. The dish is finished with a garnish of sliced chilli padi for heat and fresh curry leaves for fragrance, creating a complex interplay of savoury, umami, slightly sweet, and spicy flavours. The texture contrasts crispy prawn shells and cereal coating against the creamy, sandy salted egg yolk sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is calorie-dense at approximately 218 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (13.5 g) from the prawns, high fat (14.2 g) including saturated fat (5.8 g) from the egg yolk and butter, and moderate carbohydrates (10.8 g) from the cereal coating. Sodium content is high at approximately 620 mg per 100 g due to the salted egg yolk and seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index is estimated at 48 (low), but the caloric density warrants portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. This dish is a staple of Zi Char restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia and is commonly ordered as a shared seafood course at family-style meals.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (cooked shrimp), IMR Malaysia MyFCD (salted egg yolk sauce components), and FAO/INFOODS SEA (fried seafood analogue), with proportional weighting based on typical restaurant preparation ratios. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation methodology.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Fish and seafood', as prawns are the primary ingredient and the dish is classified as a seafood preparation. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this composite estimate. 3. All other -1 values in per_100g (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) have been replaced with estimated numeric values derived from the composite source methodology described in nutrient_source. Only vitamin_d_mcg remains at -1 as it is genuinely not reported in the source databases used. 4. The input description contained a likely OCR/typo error: 'cuTy leaves' has been interpreted as 'curry leaves'. 5. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement for this dish does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Halal status is assumed based on typical restaurant preparation using permissible ingredients; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7. Price data (SGD 38.00) from field survey data is preserved as contextual metadata but is not used as a nutritional source.

Constructed composite nutrient estimates from USDA FoodData Central, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, and FAO/INFOODS SEA sources.
Assigned GI of 48 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (battered/crumbed fried seafood).
